_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Boone County, IA. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Boone is the county seat. Records are generally intact. Coal mining company records and immigrant naturalization records are significant supplementary sources for the county's mining-era population.

previous_courthouses.fate: County seat moved to Boone when the railroad bypassed Boonesboro. Records transferred.
Replaced by current building. Records transferred intact.

record_losses.description: Boone County has no known major record loss. The county seat moved from Boonesboro to Boone when the railroad arrived, and records were transferred intact. Naturalization records are particularly significant for the Swedish, Welsh, Italian, and other immigrant mining populations.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} was a coal miner in Boone County, the naturalization records at the courthouse are a critical source — they document date and place of birth, date of immigration, and witnesses who were often fellow miners from the same community.
record_losses.source: Goldthwait, N. E. History of Boone County, Iowa. Chicago: Pioneer Publishing, 1914.

deeds.repository: Boone County Recorder; Iowa State Archives

wills_estates.completeness: Good — probate records from early settlement.
wills_estates.gaps: Mining camp residents who died intestate without property may have no probate record.
wills_estates.repository: Boone County Clerk of Court; Iowa State Archives

marriages.completeness: Good — civil marriage records from county organization.
marriages.gaps: Some mining camp marriages performed by immigrant clergy not recorded civilly.
marriages.repository: Boone County Recorder; Iowa State Archives

court_minutes.completeness: Good. Criminal court records for mining communities may document labor disputes and mining accidents.
court_minutes.gaps: Justice of the peace records incomplete.
court_minutes.repository: Boone County Courthouse; Iowa State Archives

vital_records.completeness: Iowa vital registration from 1880. Mining camp compliance was likely lower than in agricultural communities. Mining accident deaths are documented in coroner's records and company reports.
vital_records.repository: Iowa Department of Public Health; Boone County Recorder; Iowa State Archives

substitute_sources.description: Coal mining companies operating in the Des Moines River valley maintained employment records, accident reports, and payroll records. Some records survive at the State Historical Society of Iowa. The Iowa Bureau of Mines published annual reports with mine-by-mine data including accidents and employment.
substitute_sources.repository: State Historical Society of Iowa, Des Moines and Iowa City; Iowa Bureau of Mines annual reports
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Mining company records may document {ancestor_name}'s employment, wages, and living conditions. Mine accident reports name victims and sometimes witnesses. The Iowa Bureau of Mines annual reports are published and available.
substitute_sources.source: Schwieder, Dorothy. Black Diamonds: Life and Work in Iowa's Coal Mining Communities, 1895-1925. Ames: Iowa State University Press, 1983.

substitute_sources.source_type: Swedish Lutheran and other immigrant church records
substitute_sources.description: Swedish Lutheran, Welsh Congregational, and Italian Catholic congregations in the mining communities maintained records in their respective languages.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual congregations; Swedish-American Archives of Greater Chicago; Augustana Lutheran archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Church records supplement civil records for immigrant mining families. Swedish Lutheran records are particularly detailed — baptismal records often name the parish of origin in Sweden.

substitute_sources.description: Federal population schedules 1850–1940 and Iowa state censuses (1856, 1885, 1895, 1905, 1915, 1925).
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives; FamilySearch; Ancestry.com; Iowa State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Census records are essential for documenting the transient mining population. Mining camp enumerations show birthplaces, occupations, and household composition.
substitute_sources.source: Iowa State Historical Society. Iowa Census Records Guide. Des Moines: ISHS, 2005.

narrative_hooks: If {ancestor_name} was a coal miner in Boone County, the courthouse naturalization records are the first place to look — Swedish, Welsh, and Italian miners filed declarations of intent and final naturalization papers that record birthplace, immigration date, and witnesses.

The Iowa Bureau of Mines annual reports document every mine in Boone County — production, employment, and accidents — and if {ancestor_name} was injured or killed in a mine, the report is the official record.
Mining camp residents were mobile — {ancestor_name} may appear in the Boone County census for one year and in Polk or Dallas County the next, following the coal seam or the paycheck.
The county seat moved from Boonesboro to Boone when the railroad arrived — if {ancestor_name}'s earliest records say 'Boonesboro,' they are still Boone County records, filed at the same courthouse.
source_references: Goldthwait, N. E. History of Boone County, Iowa. Chicago: Pioneer Publishing, 1914.
Schwieder, Dorothy. Black Diamonds: Life and Work in Iowa's Coal Mining Communities, 1895-1925. Ames: Iowa State University Press, 1983.
Iowa State Historical Society. Iowa Census Records Guide. Des Moines: ISHS, 2005.
